agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $100K legal contract for wastewater deal
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on a second amendment to the legal services agreement with Best, Best & Krieger LLP (BBK) to advise on a new wastewater operator agreement. The amendment increases the contract by $100,000 to a total not-to-exceed $260,000 and extends the term through December 31, 2027. The current wastewater operator contract expires May 15, 2027. BBK's hourly rates for this work are: partners $450–525/hour, associates $340/hour, paralegals/clerks/analysts $205/hour. The $100,000 is funded from the Wastewater Fund budget (Account String: 40322431-400201), with no General Fund impact.

key_dates.label: Current wastewater operator contract expires
Proposed BBK amendment term ends

agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.1.a
Legal Services Agreement Amendment No. 2 with Best, Best & Krieger LLP to Advise on a Wastewater Operator Agreement
APPROVE a second amendment to the Best, Best & Krieger LLP legal services agreement to provide advice and assistance in negotiating a new wastewater operator agreement for Public Works, increasing the contract amount by $100,000 for a total payment limit of $260,000, extending the term through December 31, 2027, and AUTHORIZE the city manager or their designee to execute the amendment – City Attorney’s Office (Tawfic Halaby 510-620-5482/Shannon Moore 510-620-6509).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to approve $110K legal research contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with Thomson Reuters for legal research subscription services. The contract would cover Westlaw and CoCounsel Legal tools for the City Attorney's Office. The total cost would not exceed $110,000, running from July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2029. Council will also consider waiving the city's usual contracting rules (the Contracting and Investment Ordinance) to allow this deal to move forward. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.1.b
Contract with Thomson Reuters Enterprise for Legal Research Subscription Services
APPROVE a contract with Thomson Reuters for legal research subscription services for Westlaw and CoCounsel Legal in an amount not to exceed $110,000 for the term of July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2029, subject to non-substantive revisions approved by the City Attorney’s Office; ADOPT a resolution waiving requirements of the City’s Contracting and Investment Ordinance No. 12-18 N.S. as to this contract; and AUTHORIZE the city manager or their designee to execute the contract – City Attorney’s Office (Christopher Dykzeul 510-620-6507/Shannon Moore 510-620-6509).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $75K legal contract for HR investigations
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on a first amendment to the legal services agreement with Meyers Nave to increase the payment limit by $75,000, for a total not-to-exceed amount of $150,000, for confidential personnel investigations into discrimination, harassment, retaliation, and other workplace complaints. The term would end June 30, 2027. Funding is from the approved FY 2025-2026 Human Resources and Risk Management budgets.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

Legal Services Agreement Amendment No. 1 with Meyers Nave for Confidential Personnel Investigations
APPROVE the first amendment to the legal services agreement with Meyers Nave to increase the payment limit by $75,000, for a total amount not to exceed amount of $150,000 for a term ending June 30, 2027, for confidential personnel investigation services – City Attorney’s Office/Human Resources Department (Shannon Moore/Sharrone Taylor 510-620-6803).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $200K police review legal contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving an amendment to a legal services agreement with Aleshire & Wynder, LLP. If approved, the contract would increase by $200,000, bringing the total to $350,000. The money would pay for legal staff to support the Community Police Review Commission and for Terminal One litigation. The contract would run until December 26, 2026. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this contract will fund legal support for the police review process.

key_dates.label: Proposed legal contract end date — Community Police Review Commission

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to call Nov 3, 2026 municipal election
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a resolution to call a General Municipal Election on Tuesday, November 3, 2026, consolidated with the Statewide General Election. Candidates who advanced from the June 2 primary must file new ballot designation worksheets by July 28, 2026, and new candidate statements by August 7, 2026. Candidate statement fees are: Mayor $1,347; District 2 $330; District 3 $322; District 4 $406. Statements are limited to 250 words. Tie votes are broken by lot. Estimated election cost is $239,000.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Candidates who advanced: file ballot designation worksheet by July 28, 2026, and candidate statement by August 7, 2026, with applicable fee.

key_dates.label: Candidate ballot designation worksheet due — Richmond general election
Candidate statement and fee due — Richmond general election
Richmond General Municipal Election

agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.2.a
Calling for the November 3, 2026, General Municipal Election
ADOPT a resolution: (1) Calling and Giving Notice of the holding of a General Municipal Election to be held on Tuesday, November 3, 2026, for the election of certain officers as required by the provisions of the Charter of the City of Richmond; (2) requesting the Board of Supervisors of Contra Costa County to consolidate the General Municipal Election with the Statewide General  Election; and (3) adopting regulations for candidates pertaining to the candidates’ statements submitted to the voters, cost of the candidates’ statements and other fees, word limit for candidates’ statements; and actions to be taken for a tie vote – City Clerk’s Office (Pamela Christian 510-620-6513).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to support California refinery transparency bill
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider directing staff to send a letter of support to the California State Legislature for Senate Bill 1259. This bill would require oil refineries to publicly disclose their plans and costs for cleaning up and closing down their facilities — known as 'asset retirement obligations.' The decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting and has not been finalized yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.3.a
Support for SB 1259 – Transparency for Refinery Asset Retirement Obligations
DIRECT City Council office staff to submit a letter of support to the California State Legislature for Senate Bill 1259 on behalf of the City of Richmond – Councilmember Claudia Jimenez (510-620-6565).

attachments.title: SUPPORT SB 1259 - TRANSPARENCY FOR REFINERY ASSET RETIREMENT OBLIGATIONS.pdf
Attachment 2 -  SB 1259 - 3.11.2026 Fact sheet Transparency for Refinery Asset Retirement Obligations.pdf

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to set spending limit for 2026-27 fiscal year
agenda_briefs.summary: Council adopted a $580,294,566 appropriations limit for FY 2026-27 under Prop. 13. Separately, the Master Fee Schedule was updated with a 4.1% ECI adjustment, new fees for tobacco retail licenses ($1,460 initial, $730 annual), Fire Department permits (e.g., $905 for large-occupancy inspections), and a new non-resident recreation tier at 25% above resident rates. Developer impact fees continue a 4-year phased increase (Year 3). STMP transportation fees also rose 0.1% effective July 1, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the updated Master Fee Schedule for fee changes affecting your business; new fees take effect 30 days after June 23, 2026 (developer impact fees in 60 days).

key_dates.label: Master Fee Schedule new fees take effect
Developer impact fees take effect
STMP fee adjustment effective

agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.4.a
Fiscal Year 2026-27 Appropriations Limit
ADOPT a resolution adopting the Fiscal Year 2026-27 appropriations limit for the City of Richmond pursuant to Article XIIIB of the Constitution of the State of California – Finance Department (Emily Combs 510-620-6740/ Mubeen Qader 510-412-2077).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on new Master Fee Schedule
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council adopted a Master Fee Schedule update on June 23, 2026. Most user fees increase by 4.1% based on the Employment Cost Index. New fees include a $1,460 initial tobacco retail license ($730 annual), $905 fire inspection fee for occupancies over 20,000 sq ft, and new Tool Library fines ($1–$15/week). Developer impact fees continue Year 3 of a 4-year phased increase. Fees take effect 30 days after adoption (July 23, 2026); AB 1600 impact fees take effect 60 days after (August 22, 2026).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the new Master Fee Schedule for your business licence, permit, or tobacco retail fees; contact Finance at 510-620-6741 with questions.

key_dates.label: Master Fee Schedule takes effect (most fees)
AB 1600 developer impact fees take effect

agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.4.b
Master Fee Schedule Update
ADOPT an ordinance (second reading) establishing certain fees for the proposed Master Fee Schedule and after the second reading of the ordinance, ADOPT a resolution setting the dollar amount for the new fees and adjusting the dollar amount for current fees – Finance Department (Emily Combs/Mubeen Qader/Antonio Banuelos 510-620-6741).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond seeks applicants for city boards and commissions
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider acknowledging vacancies on City of Richmond boards, commissions, and committees as of June 23, 2026. If approved, the city will ask interested individuals to send applications to the City Clerk – Mayor’s Office. Vacancies include 6 seats on the Arts and Culture Commission, 7 on the Economic Development Commission, 5 on the Housing Advisory Commission, 4 on the Community Crisis Response Program Advisory Board, and 27 on the Workforce Development Board, among others. Term lengths range from 1 to 4 years. This is a proposed step to fill open volunteer positions that advise the city on various matters. No decision has been made yet — council will vote on whether to proceed with the call for applicants.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you want to serve on a Richmond board or commission, prepare your application now and watch for the council decision to confirm the call for applicants.

key_dates.label: Council vote on acknowledging board vacancies

agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.5.a
Boards and Commissions Vacancies Report
ACKNOWLEDGE vacancies on City of Richmond Boards, Commissions, and Committees as of June 23, 2026, and ask that interested individuals send applications to the City Clerk – Mayor’s Office (Mayor Eduardo Martinez 510-620-6503).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to accept $73K US police grant
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a resolution to accept a $73,227 grant from the 2024 Edward Byrne Memorial Justice Assistance Grant (JAG) Program. The Contra Costa County Sheriff's Office will withhold a 5% administrative fee ($3,661.35), leaving $69,565.65 for police safety equipment (ballistic vests, shields, helmets). The grant runs from October 1, 2024 to September 30, 2027. The city manager would be authorized to sign the grant agreement and handle reporting. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.

key_dates.label: JAG 2024 grant termination date — Richmond Police

agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.6.a
Accept the 2024 Edward Byrne Justice Assistance Grant
ADOPT a resolution to ACCEPT and APPROPRIATE funding in the amount of $73,227, less a five percent administrative fee, for a total reimbursable amount of $69,565.65, from the 2024 Edward Byrne Memorial Justice Assistance Grant (JAG) Program for the purchase of police safety equipment, and AUTHORIZE the city manager, or their designee, to execute the grant agreement and all necessary documents to facilitate reporting and reimbursement – Police Department (Chief Timothy Simmons 510-621-1802).

attachments.title: 2026-06-23 (reso) Edward Byrne Memorial Justice Assistance Grant (JAG) 2024 Acceptance and Appropriation.pdf
Attachment 1 – Edward Byrne Justice Assistance Grant 2024 MOU.pdf

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on $586,532 sodium hypochlorite tank replacement at wastewater plant
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposal to authorize a task authorization for Veolia to replace three 10,300-gallon sodium hypochlorite storage tanks at the Wastewater Treatment Plant. The project would cost up to $586,532, which includes a 20% contingency. The lowest responsive bid was $395,260 from W. R. Forde Associates, plus $24,291 for SCADA integration by Calcon Systems and a 16.5% Veolia markup. This is a proposed decision that has not yet been approved.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.7.a
Task Authorization for Installation of Sodium Hypochlorite Tanks at the Wastewater Treatment Plant.
ADOPT a resolution to AUTHORIZE the issuance of a Task Authorization to Veolia for the Sodium Hypochlorite Tank Replacement project in an amount not to exceed $586,532 ($488,777 plus 20% contingency) – Public Works Department (Tawfic Halaby/510-620-5482).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $39,000 environmental project contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposal to pay $24,000 to the State Water Pollution Cleanup and Abatement Account as part of a settlement. Council will also consider approving a $39,000 contract with Earth Island Institute to run a Supplemental Environmental Project through its KIDS for the BAY program. This is part of a Water Board Settlement Order (No. R2-2026-1003) — a legal agreement to resolve a pollution violation. The decision has not been made yet; it is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time — watch for the council decision.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.7.b
Water Board Settlement Payment and Supplemental Environmental Project Contract with Earth Island Institute
ADOPT a resolution to AUTHORIZE payment of $24,000 to the State Water Pollution Cleanup and Abatement Account, APPROVE a contract with Earth Island Institute for $39,000 to implement a Supplemental Environmental Project through its KIDS for the BAY project pursuant to Water Board Settlement Order No. R2-2026-1003, and AUTHORIZE the city manager or their designee to execute the contract and all necessary administrative actions - Public Works Department (Bradley Harms 510-307-8094/Tawfic Halaby 510-620-5482).

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive report on May 2026 abatement activities
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council received the May 2026 Abatement Report from Public Works. Activities included removal of 205.24 tons of illegally dumped material (252 mattresses, 167 tires, 160 gallons of paint/oil), abatement of 28 encampments (34.72 tons of debris), removal of 96 graffiti tags, weed abatement at 14 locations, and one private property abatement via warrant. Neighborhood Dumpster Days collected 38 debris and 6 recycling dumpsters, assisting 160 seniors/disabled residents. No new rules or fees are proposed.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

May 2026 Abatement Report
RECEIVE a report on the May 2026 abatement activities – Public Works Department (Daniel Chavarria 510-620-5478/Darin Fitzpatrick 510-231-3044/Hugo Mendoza 510-231-3030).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on automated transit network study
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to accept a feasibility study for an Automated Transit Network (ATN) in Richmond. The study includes three preliminary concept alternatives with planning-level capital cost ranges: Alternative 1 (Richmond Parkway Transit Center to Point Richmond, 7.4 miles, 15 stations, $1.4B–$1.7B); Alternative 2 (Richmond Parkway Transit Center to Richmond Ferry Terminal, 7.3 miles, 18 stations, $1.2B–$1.4B); and Alternative 3 (Richmond BART to El Cerrito Plaza BART, 3.7 miles, 8 stations, $0.6B–$0.7B). If approved, the city would also use approximately $100,000 in remaining study funds to install solar lighting along bus routes. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.7.d
Accept the Automated Transit Network Feasibility Study
ADOPT a resolution to ACCEPT the report on the City of Richmond Automated Transit Network Feasibility Study, including the preliminary concept alternatives and planning-level capital cost ranges; APPROPRIATE the remaining funds to install solar lighting along bus routes, and AUTHORIZE The city manager or their designee to take administrative actions as necessary – Public Works Department (Janney Lockman/Gail Payne/Daniel Chavarria 510-231-3008).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $21,500 restroom design contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving a sole-source contract with Oakley & Oakley for architectural and engineering services to remodel the Corporation Yard restroom to meet Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) standards. A 'sole-source' contract means the city plans to hire this one company without bidding it out to others. The contract would be for up to $21,500 and run from June 24, 2026 to June 24, 2028. The total revised design cost is $38,250, of which $18,764.50 has already been expended under a prior agreement. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to accept Yellow Brick Road lighting project as complete
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a resolution to accept the Yellow Brick Road Phase II Supplemental Street Lighting Project as finished. The project installed 92 new pedestrian-scale streetlights along 8th Street between Ohio Avenue and Pennsylvania Avenue at a final construction cost of $1,175,600. If approved, the city clerk would be authorized to file a Notice of Completion with the Contra Costa County recorder's office, and the city engineer would be allowed to release any bonds or retention money held for the project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to expand Lyft and Uber contracts for paratransit clients
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to existing service agreements with Lyft and Uber. If approved, the change would remove the current $200,000 annual funding limit for each company and increase the total contract value from $600,000 to $920,000 per vendor over three years. The amendment would also raise the funding for two optional one-year extensions from $200,000 to $360,000 per year for each company. This would allow the city to use ride-hailing services more flexibly for ambulatory paratransit clients — people with disabilities who can board a vehicle without a wheelchair lift. The decision has not been made yet; Council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from business owners — this is an internal city contract decision.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.7.g
Amendment for Ride Hailing Transportation Services with Lyft and Uber for Ambulatory Paratransit Clients
APPROVE an amendment to the recommended action approved on July 2, 2024, for the three-year service agreements with Lyft, Inc., and UBER Inc. (1) eliminating annual funding limits of $200,000 for total not to exceed amounts of $600,000 for each vendor and increasing the total not to exceed amount by $320,000 for a new total not to exceed amount of $920,000 with no annual funding limit; and (2) amending the two one-year extension options for each agreement from $200,000 to $360,000 for each option year per vendor - Public Works Department (Gabino Arredondo 510-620-6606).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond rental housing fee increase for 2026-27
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed resolution that would set new fees for residential rental housing units for the 2026-27 fiscal year. Under the proposal, landlords would pay $261 per fully covered (controlled) rental unit and $149 per partially covered rental unit. This fee supports the city's Rent Program, which oversees rent controls and tenant protections. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider and vote on this resolution at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council vote — if approved, this fee will apply to your rental units starting in the 2026-27 fiscal year.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
N.8.a
Establishing the Fiscal Year 2026-27 Residential Rental Housing Fee
ADOPT a resolution establishing the Fiscal Year 2026-27 Residential Rental Housing Fee in the amount of $261 per Controlled (Fully Covered) Rental Unit and $149 per Partially Covered Rental Unit – Rent Program (Fred Tran 510-620-6537).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on FY 2026-27 operating and capital budgets
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote June 23 on the proposed FY 2026-27 Operating Budget ($276.2M General Fund) and Five-Year Capital Improvement Plan (FY 2026-31). The total expenditure budget is $637,053,449. General Fund revenue is projected at $324,670,119. The capital plan includes $138,066,359 in FY 2026-27 project expenditures. A separate item directs staff to proceed with Phase 2 ballot readiness for a potential November 2026 revenue measure (parcel tax of $67/year or $120M bond) to fund Advanced Life Support paramedic services and fire station upgrades.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the June 23 council meeting or submit comments before the vote to influence how city funds are spent.

key_dates.label: Council vote on FY 2026-27 budgets
Ballot deadline for potential November 2026 revenue measure

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to consider emergency response ballot measure
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on whether to approve survey findings and proceed with Phase 2 work on a potential November 2026 ballot measure for ALS ambulance services and fire station upgrades. Separately, public hearings will be held to levy annual assessments for the Marina Bay Landscaping and Lighting Maintenance District (total $780,780) and the Hilltop Landscape Maintenance District (total $570,454.87), both with a 3% increase from FY 2025-2026. The proposed parcel tax is $67/parcel/year for 20 years; the bond measure is $120M at an estimated $40 per $100,000 of assessed valuation.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decisions on June 23, 2026 — if approved, property owners in Marina Bay and Hilltop districts will see 3% higher assessments on FY 2026-2027 tax bills.

key_dates.label: Public hearing and council vote on Marina Bay LLMD and Hilltop LMD assessments

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 3% assessment hike for Marina Bay landscaping district
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will hold a public hearing to consider approving a 3% increase in annual assessments for the Marina Bay Landscaping and Lighting Maintenance District for the 2026-2027 fiscal year. If approved, property owners in the district would pay higher fees to cover landscaping and lighting maintenance costs. The proposed increase is part of the Engineer's Report that Council will vote on after the hearing.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing to voice your support or concerns about the proposed 3% assessment increase.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 3% assessment increase for Hilltop Landscape Maintenance District
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will hold a public hearing to consider adopting a resolution that would approve the Engineer’s Report and order the levy and collection of annual assessments for the Hilltop Landscape Maintenance District for Fiscal Year 2026-2027. The total proposed assessment is $570,454.87, a three percent increase from Fiscal Year 2025-2026. The district covers residential, commercial, industrial, and vacant properties near Hilltop Mall, Richmond Parkway, Atlas Road, and surrounding areas. This is not yet approved — the public hearing is the chance for affected owners to have their say before council votes.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing to voice your support or concerns about the proposed 3% assessment increase.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond policy on internet disruptions during council meetings
agenda_briefs.summary: The City of Richmond is acknowledging vacancies on 15 boards, commissions, and committees as of June 23, 2026. Interested residents can apply to the City Clerk for seats on bodies including the Arts and Culture Commission (6 vacancies), Economic Development Commission (7 vacancies), Housing Advisory Commission (5 vacancies), and others. Term lengths range from 1 to 4 years. Applications are accepted via the City Clerk's website or by phone.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit an application to the Richmond City Clerk for any vacant board or commission seat.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $50K housing equity contract amendment
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed second contract amendment with Just Cities, LLC. If approved, this amendment would increase the contract's payment limit by $49,999 (for a new total of $209,994) and extend the contract term through December 31, 2027. The purpose of this contract is to complete and implement the 'Housing Equity Roadmap,' which is part of the city's Housing Element — a plan to make housing more fair and accessible for all residents. This is a proposed decision and has not yet been approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect future housing development and equity programs in Richmond.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:

Contract Amendment No. 2 with Just Cities, LLC for completion of the Housing Equity Roadmap as specified in the Housing Element
APPROVE a second contract amendment with Just Cities, LLC to support completion and implementation of the Housing Equity Road Map increasing the payment limit by $49,999, for a total payment limit of $209,994, and extending the term through December 31, 2027– Community Development Department (Lina Velasco 510-620-6841/Jesus Morales 510-620-6649).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Contract for graffiti removal from historic concrete building
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving a contract with All Star Painting KD Inc. to remove graffiti from the historic concrete surfaces of the General Warehouse Building at Point Potrero Marine Terminal. The proposed contract would run from June 1, 2026 to December 30, 2027, and cost no more than $87,777. Work includes graffiti removal tests at four locations, removal from all exterior surfaces including doors and loading docks, and application of anti-graffiti coating to 15,000 square feet. All work must adhere to Secretary of the Interior's Standards; abrasive blasting and pressure washing over 500 psi are prohibited. Contractor must complete work within 20 working days of Notice to Proceed. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.3
Contract with All Star Painting KD Inc. for Graffiti Removal of Historic Concrete
ADOPT a resolution to APPROVE a contract with All Star Painting KD Inc. for graffiti removal from the historic concrete surfaces of the General Warehouse Building, for a term expiring December 31, 2027, in an amount not to exceed $87,777 – Economic Development Department/Port (Charles Gerard 510-620-6792).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Road closure at Glenn and McBryde Avenues for park safety project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed roadway modification for the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project. If approved, the direct vehicle connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue would be closed. The goal is to improve traffic safety, reduce cars cutting through the neighbourhood, and make it safer and easier for people walking and biking to get to the park. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this road closure could change traffic patterns near your business.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:

Roadway Modification at Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue for the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project
ADOPT a resolution to APPROVE a roadway modification associated with the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project, including closure of the direct vehicular connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue to improve traffic safety, reduce cut-through traffic, and support pedestrian and bicycle connectivity – Public Works Department (Hillal Hamdan 510-621-1612/Daniel Chavarria 510-620-5478).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to explore regional drone show for July 3rd at Hilltop Mall
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal to direct the city manager to contact neighbouring municipalities (Pinole, San Pablo) and explore 'piggybacking' on an existing municipal contract to secure discounted regional pricing for a drone light show. The show would be held at Hilltop Mall on July 3rd. The city manager would report back with findings by June 23, 2026, so council can make a final decision. This is not approved yet; it is up for discussion and a vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you now — watch for the council decision on June 23, 2026, which could affect local event opportunities.

key_dates.label: Council decision deadline on drone show proposal

agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.5
Explore Regional Partnerships and "Piggyback" Contracting Opportunities for a July 3rd Drone Light Show at Hilltop Mall
DIRECT the city manager to contact neighboring municipalities to identify regional 4th of July drone show contracts, assess the feasibility of "piggybacking" onto an existing municipal vendor agreement to secure discounted regional pricing, and explore hosting a sustainable drone light show at Hilltop Mall on July 3rd at a discounted price and bring back findings for a City Council decision by June 23, 2026 - Councilmember Soheila Bana (510-672-3490).

attachments.title: Explore Regional Partnerships and Piggyback Contracting Opportunities for a July 3rd Drone Light Show at Hilltop Mall.pdf

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project up for council direction
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider prioritizing the 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project, a temporary street-closure event on Saturday, September 12, 2026, from 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM between Barrett Avenue and Rheem Avenue (0.8-mile segment). Preliminary cost estimates range from $79,000 to $145,000. The event would extend street closures, limit business loading/deliveries, and require AC Transit detours.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council direction — if approved, businesses on 23rd Street between Barrett and Rheem Avenues will face extended street closures and restricted loading/deliveries on Sept 12, 2026.

key_dates.label: 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot event date

agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.6
23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project
RECEIVE an update on the proposed 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project and PROVIDE DIRECTION to staff regarding whether this effort should be prioritized within the current Transportation and Public Works work plan, including consideration of staffing capacity, cost, trade-offs with existing Council-directed priorities, and impacts to ongoing traffic safety, traffic calming, grant-funded project delivery, and resident/Council traffic-related requests - Public Works Department (Gail Payne 510-620-6716/Daniel Chavarria 510-620-5478).
